content.full_text:
Del Puerto Hospital
Patterson's Not-For-Profit District Hospital

P.O. Box 187
Patterson, CA 95363
(209) 892-8781

February 13, 1990

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request for Informal Assistance Regarding Conflict of Interest

Gentlemen:

As President of the Board of Directors of the Patterson Hospital
District I wish to request informal assistance concerning a
member of our Board, Walter Rea, Sr. The following three ques-
tions are posed for written response pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b) and California Administrative Code, Title 2,
Division 6, Section 18329(c). As President of the Board, con-
ducting meetings, I have "a duty to advise other persons relating
to their duties or actions under the Act". Specifically, I
believe that I will be required to rule upon whether or not this
Board Member has a conflict of interest which would require that
he (a) not participate in, or (b) vote on, or (c) both in certain
decisions which must necessarily be made by the Board of Directors.

FACTS:

Mr. Rea is a member of the Board of Directors of the Patterson
Hospital District. Patterson Hospital District owns a parcel of
real property adjacent to the District's hospital facilities.
The District sought to develop a medical office complex on the
parcel, and applied to the City of Patterson for zoning approval.
During a public meeting of the City Council at which the project
was considered, Mr. Rea, who was opposed to the project, offered
to buy the parcel for $5.00 more than the best offer the hospital
might receive. He further offered to donate the property to the
City provided the City would commit to the use of the subject
property for a Senior Citizen Center. A copy of his comments are
appended herein. The City denied approval of the proposed hos-
pital development later at the same public meeting.

Local efforts are currently underway by private citizens to over-
turn the city's denial through circulation of a Ballot Title and
Summary pursuant to Elections Code Section 4001-4011. Mr. Rea
has continued to speak publicly against the hospital's pro-
posed project in response to this citizen effort. Pending the
outcome of the public petition effort, the Hospital District may
be required to consider further action related to the project.

A Public Entity Providing Health Care Services

QUESTION ONE:

Is Mr. Rea henceforth prevented from participating in decisions
regarding the currently-owned real property since he made an
offer to purchase the parcel for $5.00 more than the best offer
the district might receive, and then offered to give the parcel
to the City for a Senior Citizen Center?

QUESTION TWO:

Is Mr. Rea henceforth prevented from speaking publically on
issues related to the disposition or use of the real property in
question since his comments might tend to influence the outcome?

FACTS:

Mr. Rea is also President of the Stanislaus County Commission
on Aging. The duties and powers of local commissioners on aging
are set forth in Welfare & Institutions Code Section 9351.
Essentially such commissions are to advocate the needs of older
persons within the community, a number of which are health re-
lated.

Hospital districts are empowered to hold property, exercise
eminent domain, operate health facilities, and do any and all
acts to carry out the local hospital district law. Health &
Safety Code Section 32121. The purpose of the local hospital
district law is to protect the public health and welfare by
furnishing hospital facilities and health care services. As
noted above, Mr. Rea publically proposed that the ultimate use of
real property owned by the hospital district be that of a Senior
Citizen Center.

QUESTION THREE:

Are the offices of Hospital District Board member and President
of a county commission on aging incompatible offices?

We request that your office render written advice to the questions
presented in this letter within 21 working days as provided in
Government Code Section 83114(b) and California Administrative
Code, Title 2, Division 6, Section 18329(c)(2).

If you have any questions regarding this request, I may personally
be reached at (209) 862-1504, or you may speak with Thomas Lynn
Avery, Administrator of Del Puerto Hospital at (209) 892-8781

Thank you very much.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Steve Pedrazzi
President, Board of Directors
Patterson Hospital District

I would like to speak to what I believe are some of the central issues posed here
tonight.

They are, the future of Del Puerto Hospital, the sale of some of its assets, its
future location, the direction of the City and its plans, and meeting the needs
and fears of the local residents surrounding the present Hospital location.

A. To my knowledge, no one wants to see Del Puerto Hospital close, we need the
Hospital and want it. If it survives, we all prosper, if it fails we all suffer.
What is at issue is what method we should use to insure its survival.

B. The land that the Hospital sits on does not belong to the Hospital Adminis-
tration, or the Hospital Board, or the City of Patterson. It is owned by the people
of the hospital district, and as such must be subject to their wishes as we are able
to perceive them. To sell to private interest for private profit could be conceived
by outside enforcing agencies, such as a County Grand Jury, Fair Political Practice
Committee, or State District Attorney as a sweetheart deal or conflict of interest.
We do not need or want the publicity of public scrutiny at this time. As to Attorneys
opinions for or against some conflict of interest, Attorney opinions are just that,
opinions. For every Attorney quoted on one side of an issue, you can obtain an
opposite opinion on the same issue from another Attorney. That is why we have the
court system in the country to settle Attorneys opinions.

C. There has been no evidence presented to me as a board member that selling
hospital land will bring in business to the hospital. Doctors that rent a building
where ever it is located that have an association or affiliation with another hospital
are going to be loyal to that first hospital not Del Puerto Hospital.

D. I can never remember a time when selling off land of an institution was a
good idea, but only regretted in the future.

E. Indeed, the issue seems to be that the Administrator would like a new hospital.
Of course, we all would and no Administrator should be faulted because he is trying
to sell his product. But the real question is, do we have a product to sell? A
building and a new location does not guarantee a viable hospital.

F. What then in the light of these facts is my answer to this problem?

1. I ask the city council to vote no on the sale of public land to private
interest for private profit.

2. If, as the Administrator of the Hospital has stated, the land is open for
public bid, I make the following proposal.

A. I will bid $5.00 more then the private group for the land.
That money to be deposited in Public Escrow for the hospital at the
rate of $5,000 each year for 12 years at interest.

B. In turn for that sale, I will donate that land to the City of
Patterson, if they will commit it and a building to be a Senior Center
for Patterson. As President of the Stanislaus County Commission on Aging,
I know that Federal' and State Grants are available for such a project and
should have been used for such a project long ago.

C. I then would urge the City of Patterson to consider moving their
City Hall and operation to the Hospital location should the hospital be
moved at some future time.

I believe that this proposal would help the hospital more than selling the land
for private profit. It would be a help to the Seniors of our area, as well as the
Seniors that are yet to come. It would relieve the fears of local residents around
the hospital and bring stability to the area. It could give a new direction to
City Planning and finally would provide private funds for the future expansion of
the hospital should that happen. I hope you will give my plan serious thought.

sections.question:
ONE:

Is Mr. Rea henceforth prevented from participating in decisions regarding the currently-owned real property since he made an offer to purchase the parcel for $5.00 more than the best offer the district might receive, and then offered to give the parcel to the City for a Senior Citizen Center?

QUESTION TWO:

Is Mr. Rea henceforth prevented from speaking publically on issues related to the disposition or use of the real property in question since his comments might tend to influence the outcome?

FACTS:

Mr. Rea is also President of the Stanislaus County Commission on Aging. The duties and powers of local commissioners on aging are set forth in Welfare & Institutions Code Section 9351. Essentially such commissions are to advocate the needs of older persons within the community, a number of which are health related.

Hospital districts are empowered to hold property, exercise eminent domain, operate health facilities, and do any and all acts to carry out the local hospital district law. Health & Safety Code Section 32121. The purpose of the local hospital district law is to protect the public health and welfare by furnishing hospital facilities and health care services. As noted above, Mr. Rea publically proposed that the ultimate use of real property owned by the hospital district be that of a Senior Citizen Center.

QUESTION THREE:

Are the offices of Hospital District Board member and President of a county commission on aging incompatible offices?

We request that your office render written advice to the questions presented in this letter within 21 working days as provided in Government Code Section 83114(b) and California Administrative Code, Title 2, Division 6, Section 18329(c)(2).
sections.facts:
Mr. Rea is a member of the Board of Directors of the Patterson Hospital District. Patterson Hospital District owns a parcel of real property adjacent to the District's hospital facilities. The District sought to develop a medical office complex on the parcel, and applied to the City of Patterson for zoning approval. During a public meeting of the City Council at which the project was considered, Mr. Rea, who was opposed to the project, offered to buy the parcel for $5.00 more than the best offer the hospital might receive. He further offered to donate the property to the City provided the City would commit to the use of the subject property for a Senior Citizen Center. A copy of his comments are appended herein. The City denied approval of the proposed hospital development later at the same public meeting.

Local efforts are currently underway by private citizens to overturn the city's denial through circulation of a Ballot Title and Summary pursuant to Elections Code Section 4001-4011. Mr. Rea has continued to speak out publically against the hospital's proposed project in response to this citizen effort. Pending the outcome of the public petition effort, the Hospital District may be required to consider further action related to the project.
